penicillin prophylaxis in children to prevent pneumococcal infection
vaccinations: pneumococcal, meningococcal, influenza, hepatitis B
L-glutamine for reducing pain episodes
voxelotor and crizanlizumab as newer targeted therapies
iron chelation therapy if transfusion-related iron overload
bone marrow or stem cell transplant for eligible patients
annual screening: transcranial Doppler, retinal exam, kidney function
genetic counseling for family planning

symptoms: redness and swelling of the affected joint
most commonly affects the big toe
peeling skin around the joint as it heals
recommended_questions: Which joint is affected?
When did the pain start? Was it sudden?
Is this the first attack or have you had similar episodes before?
Is the joint red, swollen, and warm?
Can you bear any pressure on the joint?
What is your diet like? Do you eat red meat, organ meats, or shellfish?
How much alcohol do you drink? Especially beer?
Do you drink sugary beverages?
Are you taking any diuretics or other medications?
Do you have kidney disease?
Have you had your uric acid level checked?
Do you have any lumps under your skin near joints?
Do you have a family history of gout?
Have you been under any physiological stress: surgery, illness, dehydration?
treatments: NSAIDs such as indomethacin or naproxen for acute attacks
colchicine within the first 36 hours of an attack
corticosteroids if NSAIDs and colchicine contraindicated
rest and ice the affected joint during acute attacks
urate-lowering therapy with allopurinol for recurrent gout
febuxostat as alternative to allopurinol
target serum urate below 6 mg/dL
dietary modifications: reduce purine-rich foods and alcohol
increase fluid intake
weight management
avoid triggers: dehydration, fasting, binge eating
prophylaxis with low-dose colchicine when starting urate-lowering therapy
